title: Psalms 49
subtitle: The Redemption of a Soul Is Costly

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — “No One Can Redeem the Life of Another”

heb.paragraph: "No one can redeem (fedeh) the life of another or give to God a ransom for them — the ransom for a life is costly, no payment is ever enough" (vv.7–8). A wisdom psalm on death and wealth. Wise and foolish alike die (v.10). Their graves are their eternal homes (v.11). No amount of wealth can buy off death. "People who have wealth but lack understanding are like the beasts that perish" (v.12).

notes.note: "No one can redeem the life of another. The ransom for a life is costly, no payment is ever enough — so that they should live on forever and not see decay." Human wealth cannot purchase immortality. Only God can pay the ransom — the cross is the answer this psalm cannot yet see.

"Wise and foolish alike die. Their tombs will remain their houses forever. People who have wealth but lack understanding are like beasts that perish." Death is the great equaliser, echoing Ecclesiastes 2:14–16.

sections.header: Verses 13–20 — “But God Will Redeem Me from the Realm of the Dead”

heb.paragraph: "But God will redeem me (yifdēnî) from the realm of the dead; he will surely take me to himself" (v.15). The psalm’s turning point. No HUMAN can pay the ransom (v.7), but GOD will redeem from Sheol. The word "take" (lāqaḥ) is the same verb used for Enoch’s translation (Gen 5:24) and Elijah’s assumption (2 Kgs 2:3). God will take the psalmist beyond death.

notes.note: "But God will redeem me from the realm of the dead; he will surely take me to himself." The psalm’s theological centre. What humans cannot do (v.7), God does (v.15). The ransom no person can pay, God pays. The cross is the ransom.

"Do not be overawed when others grow rich. For they will take nothing with them when they die." The conclusion echoes Job 1:21 and Ecclesiastes 5:15: naked we came, naked we depart. But the redeemed have v.15: God takes them to himself.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Only God can pay the ransom — the cross is the answer this psalm cannot yet see…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWhy should I fear when evil days come, when wicked deceivers surround me—ESVWhy should I fear in times of trouble, when the iniquity of those who cheat me surrounds me,NIVMy mouth will speak words of wisdom; the meditation of my heart will give you understanding.ESVMy mouth shall speak wisdom; the meditation of my heart shall be understanding.

rec.text: ""No one can redeem the life of another. The ransom for a life is costly, no payment is ever enough — so that they should live on forever and not see decay." Human wealth cannot purchase immortality. O…"

rows.label: No Human Ransom — 49:1–12
rows.text: "No one can redeem another." The ransom is too costly. Wise and fool alike die.

hebtext.note: "No one can redeem (fedeh) the life of another or give to God a ransom for them — the ransom for a life is costly, no payment is ever enough" (vv.7–8). A wisdom psalm on death and wealth. Wise and foolish alike die (v.10). Their graves are their eternal homes (v.11). No amount of wealth can buy off death. "People who have wealth but lack understanding are like the beasts that perish" (v.12).

"But God will redeem me (yifdēnî) from the realm of the dead; he will surely take me to himself" (v.15). The psalm’s turning point. No HUMAN can pay the ransom (v.7), but GOD will redeem from Sheol. The word "take" (lāqaḥ) is the same verb used for Enoch’s translation (Gen 5:24) and Elijah’s assumption (2 Kgs 2:3). God will take the psalmist beyond death.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks: the ransom no human can pay + God’s redemption from Sheol anticipates the cross. Mercy: divine ransom. Justice: death equalises all.
